quiz_questions.question_text: Graded potentials differ from action potentials in that graded potentials are:

options.text: Only found in muscle cells, not neurons
All-or-none signals that always reach maximum amplitude
Self-propagating signals that travel long distances without losing strength
Local, decremental potentials that can be either excitatory or inhibitory and vary in amplitude

quiz_questions.explanation: Graded potentials (including EPSPs and IPSPs) are local changes in membrane potential that vary in magnitude based on stimulus strength, decrease with distance from the point of origin, and do not follow the all-or-none principle like action potentials.

quiz_questions.question_text: Temporal summation occurs when:

options.text: Neurotransmitter accumulates in the synaptic cleft over many hours
Multiple presynaptic neurons fire onto the same postsynaptic neuron simultaneously
The postsynaptic neuron sends signals backward to the presynaptic neuron
A single presynaptic neuron fires rapidly so that successive graded potentials overlap and add together before they decay

quiz_questions.explanation: Temporal summation occurs when a single presynaptic neuron fires in rapid succession, so each new EPSP arrives before the previous one has fully decayed, allowing the postsynaptic potentials to stack and potentially reach threshold.

quiz_questions.question_text: Spatial summation occurs when:

options.text: Graded potentials travel from one neuron to the next without a synapse
A single large EPSP triggers an action potential on its own
Multiple presynaptic neurons stimulate the same postsynaptic neuron simultaneously, and their graded potentials add together
One presynaptic neuron fires repeatedly over time

quiz_questions.explanation: Spatial summation occurs when graded potentials from multiple presynaptic neurons converge on the same postsynaptic cell simultaneously, combining their effects at the axon hillock to determine whether threshold is reached.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Na+/K+ pump is classified as active transport because it:

options.text: Uses ATP to move Na+ and K+ against their concentration gradients to maintain the resting potential
Moves ions down their concentration gradients
Does not require any energy input
Operates through ion channels that open and close passively

quiz_questions.explanation: The Na+/K+ ATPase is a primary active transporter that uses ATP energy to pump 3 Na+ ions out and 2 K+ ions in against their concentration gradients, maintaining the electrochemical gradient essential for the resting membrane potential and action potential generation.

quiz_questions.question_text: The neurotransmitter at the somatic neuromuscular junction (NMJ) is always:

options.text: GABA
Acetylcholine (ACh)
Dopamine
Norepinephrine

quiz_questions.explanation: Acetylcholine is the sole neurotransmitter at all somatic neuromuscular junctions, where it binds to nicotinic receptors on the skeletal muscle membrane (motor end plate) to initiate muscle contraction.

quiz_questions.question_text: During the absolute refractory period, a neuron:

options.text: Is at its resting membrane potential and ready to fire
Cannot generate a new action potential regardless of stimulus strength because Na+ channels are inactivated
Is undergoing depolarization
Can fire an action potential with a stronger-than-normal stimulus

quiz_questions.explanation: During the absolute refractory period, voltage-gated Na+ channels are in their inactivated state and cannot be reopened, making it physically impossible to generate another action potential regardless of how strong the stimulus is.

quiz_questions.question_text: During the relative refractory period:

options.text: A stronger-than-normal stimulus can trigger an action potential because some Na+ channels have returned to their resting state
No action potential is possible under any circumstance
The neuron is fully recovered and fires at normal threshold
The membrane potential is at +30 mV

quiz_questions.explanation: During the relative refractory period, some Na+ channels have recovered from inactivation, but K+ channels may still be open, so a stronger-than-normal stimulus is required to reach threshold and trigger an action potential.

quiz_questions.question_text: Nerve impulse conduction velocity is fastest in:

options.text: Small-diameter, unmyelinated fibers
Cell bodies in the gray matter
Large-diameter, heavily myelinated fibers
Dendrites of any size

quiz_questions.explanation: Conduction velocity is determined by two main factors: axon diameter (larger = less internal resistance = faster) and myelination (enables saltatory conduction), with the fastest fibers (Type A-alpha) conducting at up to 120 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: The direction of action potential propagation along an axon is determined by:

options.text: Gravity pulling ions down the axon
The direction of blood flow near the nerve
The refractory period, which prevents the impulse from traveling backward into recently depolarized regions
The physical shape and curvature of the axon

quiz_questions.explanation: Unidirectional propagation is ensured by the refractory period: the region of axon membrane just behind the advancing action potential is in its refractory state and cannot be restimulated, so the impulse can only move forward toward the axon terminal.

quiz_questions.question_text: The hypothalamus connects the nervous and endocrine systems primarily by:

options.text: Sending motor neurons to the adrenal glands
Controlling the pituitary gland through releasing and inhibiting hormones and direct neural connections
Producing cerebrospinal fluid that contains hormones
Directly secreting hormones into the general bloodstream from its own neurons

quiz_questions.explanation: The hypothalamus serves as the crucial link between the nervous and endocrine systems by secreting releasing/inhibiting hormones into the hypophyseal portal system to control the anterior pituitary, and by sending axons directly to the posterior pituitary to release oxytocin and ADH.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a graded potential?

options.text: The resting membrane potential maintained by the Na+/K+ pump
An all-or-none electrical signal that propagates along the entire length of the axon
A local change in membrane potential that varies in magnitude depending on stimulus strength and decreases with distance from the point of stimulation
The threshold voltage required to trigger a nerve impulse

quiz_questions.explanation: A graded potential is a localized, variable-amplitude change in membrane potential (either depolarizing or hyperpolarizing) that is proportional to stimulus intensity and decays over short distances, serving as the input signal that may or may not reach threshold to trigger an action potential.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'electrochemical gradient' mean?

options.text: The flow of electricity through the brain measured by an EEG
The chemical reactions that produce neurotransmitters in the cell body
The speed at which an action potential travels along a myelinated axon
The combined driving force on an ion created by both its concentration gradient and the electrical charge difference across the membrane

quiz_questions.explanation: The electrochemical gradient is the combination of two forces acting on ions: the chemical concentration gradient (ions move from high to low concentration) and the electrical gradient (ions move toward opposite charges), which together determine the direction and magnitude of ion flow across the membrane.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the axon hillock?

options.text: A specialized receptor on the dendrite surface
The gap between two adjacent myelin segments
The terminal end of the axon where neurotransmitter is released
The region where the axon emerges from the cell body, serving as the trigger zone where action potentials are initiated

quiz_questions.explanation: The axon hillock is the cone-shaped region at the junction of the cell body and axon, containing the highest density of voltage-gated Na+ channels, making it the trigger zone where EPSPs and IPSPs are integrated and where action potentials are initiated if threshold is reached.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'voltage-gated channel' mean?

options.text: An ion channel that is always open regardless of conditions
An ion channel that opens or closes in response to changes in the membrane potential
An ion pump that uses ATP to move ions against their gradient
An ion channel that only opens when a neurotransmitter binds to it

quiz_questions.explanation: Voltage-gated channels are membrane proteins that change conformation (open or close) in response to changes in the electrical potential across the membrane, such as the voltage-gated Na+ and K+ channels critical for action potential generation and propagation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an electroencephalogram (EEG)?

options.text: A non-invasive recording of the brain's electrical activity using electrodes placed on the scalp, used to diagnose epilepsy and sleep disorders
A recording of the electrical activity of the heart
An image of brain structure produced by magnetic resonance
A test that measures the speed of nerve conduction in peripheral nerves

quiz_questions.explanation: An EEG records the summed electrical activity of millions of cortical neurons through scalp electrodes, producing waveforms (alpha, beta, theta, delta waves) that are essential for diagnosing epilepsy, sleep disorders, brain death, and monitoring anesthesia depth.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'nerve conduction velocity' mean?

options.text: The rate at which neurotransmitter is released at the synapse
The number of action potentials a neuron can generate per second
The speed at which an electrical impulse travels along a nerve fiber, measured in meters per second
The distance between nodes of Ranvier on a myelinated axon

quiz_questions.explanation: Nerve conduction velocity is the speed at which an action potential propagates along a nerve fiber (measured in m/s), determined primarily by axon diameter and myelination; nerve conduction studies measure this clinically to diagnose conditions like peripheral neuropathy and demyelinating diseases.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'ligand-gated channel' mean?

options.text: An active transport pump that moves ions using ATP
An ion channel that opens in response to changes in membrane voltage
An ion channel that opens when a specific chemical messenger (ligand) such as a neurotransmitter binds to it
An ion channel that is mechanically opened by physical pressure

quiz_questions.explanation: Ligand-gated channels (also called chemically-gated channels) are ion channels that open when a specific chemical ligand (such as acetylcholine or GABA) binds to a receptor site on the channel, allowing specific ions to flow through and generating graded postsynaptic potentials.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the equilibrium potential of an ion?

options.text: The maximum speed at which the ion can cross the membrane
The point at which all ion channels for that ion are closed
The membrane potential at which there is no net movement of that ion across the membrane because the electrical and chemical driving forces are equal and opposite
The concentration of the ion on both sides of the membrane being exactly equal

quiz_questions.explanation: The equilibrium potential (calculated by the Nernst equation) is the membrane voltage at which the electrical gradient exactly balances the concentration gradient for a specific ion, resulting in no net flux of that ion across the membrane (e.g., approximately -90 mV for K+ and +60 mV for Na+).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a neuromuscular junction (NMJ)?

options.text: The point where a peripheral nerve enters the spinal cord
The connection between a sensory receptor and its afferent neuron
The junction where two bones meet at a joint
The specialized synapse between a motor neuron's axon terminal and a skeletal muscle fiber where acetylcholine triggers muscle contraction

quiz_questions.explanation: The neuromuscular junction is the specialized chemical synapse where a motor neuron's axon terminal meets the motor end plate of a skeletal muscle fiber; ACh released here binds to nicotinic receptors, generating an end-plate potential that triggers muscle contraction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'convergence' mean in neural circuits?

options.text: One presynaptic neuron sending signals to many postsynaptic neurons
Multiple presynaptic neurons sending their signals to a single postsynaptic neuron, allowing integration of diverse inputs
A nerve signal traveling in two directions at once
Two neurons firing simultaneously to create a synchronized rhythm

quiz_questions.explanation: Convergence is a neural circuit pattern where multiple presynaptic neurons synapse on a single postsynaptic neuron, allowing that neuron to integrate information from many sources through summation of EPSPs and IPSPs before deciding whether to fire.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physiology student is given a graph showing a neuron's membrane potential over time. The trace shows the potential rising from -70 mV to -55 mV, then rapidly spiking to +30 mV, dropping past -70 mV to -80 mV, and finally returning to -70 mV. The student is asked to identify each phase. What is the correct sequence of events?

options.text: Hyperpolarization, threshold, repolarization, depolarization, resting
Depolarization only; the other changes are artifact
Repolarization, depolarization, resting potential, hyperpolarization
Resting potential to threshold (graded potential), depolarization (Na+ influx to +30 mV), repolarization (K+ efflux past -70 mV), hyperpolarization (undershoot to -80 mV), return to resting potential

quiz_questions.explanation: The action potential phases are: resting potential (-70 mV) rising to threshold (-55 mV) via graded potentials, rapid depolarization to +30 mV (Na+ channel opening), repolarization back toward -70 mV (K+ channel opening, Na+ inactivation), hyperpolarization undershoot to ~-80 mV (excess K+ efflux), and gradual return to resting potential.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher applies tetrodotoxin (TTX, from puffer fish) to a neuron in a laboratory experiment. TTX specifically blocks voltage-gated Na+ channels. What would the researcher observe on an oscilloscope when attempting to stimulate this neuron?

options.text: Action potentials that are twice the normal amplitude
Continuous depolarization without repolarization
No action potentials can be generated because Na+ influx during depolarization is completely blocked
Normal action potentials at double the usual frequency

quiz_questions.explanation: TTX blocks voltage-gated Na+ channels, which are essential for the depolarization phase of the action potential. Without Na+ influx, the membrane cannot depolarize to generate an action potential, resulting in complete nerve block, similar to how local anesthetics work.

quiz_questions.question_text: An AP exam question asks: 'If the extracellular K+ concentration is experimentally increased from 5 mM to 15 mM, what happens to the resting membrane potential and neuronal excitability?' What is the correct answer?

options.text: The resting potential becomes more negative (hyperpolarized) and the neuron becomes less excitable
The resting potential is unchanged because K+ has no effect on membrane potential
The resting potential becomes less negative (partially depolarized) and the neuron becomes more excitable because it is closer to threshold
The neuron immediately fires a continuous stream of action potentials

quiz_questions.explanation: Increasing extracellular K+ reduces the K+ concentration gradient across the membrane, meaning less K+ flows out through leak channels, resulting in a less negative (depolarized) resting potential. This brings the membrane closer to threshold, making the neuron hyperexcitable. This is clinically relevant in hyperkalemia.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pharmacology student is studying how different drugs affect synaptic transmission. Drug A blocks the reuptake of serotonin. Drug B inhibits acetylcholinesterase. Drug C blocks GABA receptors. Which drug would most likely cause seizures, and why?

options.text: Drug B, because excess ACh at the NMJ causes muscle convulsions that look like seizures
Drug C, because blocking GABA (the main inhibitory neurotransmitter) removes inhibition from brain circuits, leading to uncontrolled excitatory firing and seizures
None of these drugs could cause seizures
Drug A, because increased serotonin causes neuronal overexcitation

quiz_questions.explanation: Drug C (GABA receptor blocker) would most likely cause seizures because GABA is the brain's principal inhibitory neurotransmitter. Blocking GABA receptors removes the normal inhibitory brake on neural circuits, allowing unchecked excitatory (glutamatergic) activity that leads to synchronized, excessive neuronal firing characteristic of seizures.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with myasthenia gravis is given neostigmine, an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor, and her muscle strength temporarily improves. A student asks how inhibiting the enzyme that breaks down ACh helps strengthen muscle contractions.

options.text: By inhibiting acetylcholinesterase, neostigmine prevents ACh breakdown, increasing its concentration and duration in the synaptic cleft, compensating for the reduced number of functional receptors
Neostigmine stops the autoimmune attack on ACh receptors
Neostigmine increases the number of ACh receptors on the muscle
Neostigmine directly stimulates the muscle fibers without needing ACh

quiz_questions.explanation: In myasthenia gravis, autoantibodies destroy ACh receptors, so fewer receptors are available. Neostigmine inhibits acetylcholinesterase, preventing ACh breakdown and allowing ACh to remain in the synaptic cleft longer, giving it more time and opportunity to bind the remaining receptors and produce a stronger end-plate potential.

quiz_questions.question_text: An AP exam scenario describes a neuron receiving 10 EPSPs and 8 IPSPs simultaneously. The net effect is a small depolarization that does not reach threshold. What will happen at the axon hillock?

options.text: No action potential will fire because the net summation did not reach threshold, but the neuron is slightly more excitable than at rest
An action potential will fire because there are more EPSPs than IPSPs
The neuron will become permanently hyperpolarized
The neuron will fire at half its normal amplitude

quiz_questions.explanation: The axon hillock integrates all incoming EPSPs (depolarizing) and IPSPs (hyperpolarizing) through summation. Although the EPSPs numerically outnumber the IPSPs, the net depolarization is subthreshold, so no action potential fires. The all-or-none principle means no 'partial' AP is possible.

quiz_questions.question_text: A clinical neuroscience exam asks why general anesthesia works to render patients unconscious and unable to feel pain during surgery. Based on your understanding of neurophysiology, which mechanism best explains general anesthesia?

options.text: General anesthetics work by reducing blood flow to the brain
General anesthetics only block peripheral pain receptors while the brain remains fully active
General anesthetics enhance inhibitory neurotransmission (GABA) and/or block excitatory transmission (glutamate/NMDA), broadly suppressing CNS neural activity
General anesthetics permanently destroy sensory neurons so pain signals cannot be transmitted

quiz_questions.explanation: Most general anesthetics work through dual mechanisms: enhancing GABA-mediated inhibition (increasing Cl- influx to hyperpolarize neurons) and blocking glutamate/NMDA-mediated excitation, resulting in widespread suppression of CNS activity that produces unconsciousness, amnesia, analgesia, and muscle relaxation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A neuroscience researcher discovers a new toxin that permanently opens K+ channels on a neuron. What effect would this toxin have on the neuron's ability to fire action potentials?

options.text: The neuron would depolarize to +30 mV and stay there
The neuron would become hyperpolarized (more negative than resting potential) because of excessive K+ efflux, making it very difficult or impossible to reach threshold and fire
The neuron would fire action potentials continuously
There would be no effect on the action potential

quiz_questions.explanation: Permanently open K+ channels would cause continuous K+ efflux, hyperpolarizing the membrane well below the resting potential. This would make it extremely difficult for any excitatory input to depolarize the membrane to threshold, effectively silencing the neuron.

quiz_questions.question_text: An AP exam question presents two patients: Patient A has damage to the sympathetic chain ganglia, and Patient B has damage to the vagus nerve. When both patients exercise, which one would have a more dangerous cardiac response, and why?

options.text: Both patients would have identical cardiac responses during exercise
Patient A is in more danger because without sympathetic input, the heart cannot increase its rate during exercise to meet metabolic demands
Neither patient would experience any cardiac changes during exercise
Patient B is in more danger because without the vagus nerve, the heart would beat too slowly during exercise

quiz_questions.explanation: Patient A (sympathetic damage) cannot adequately increase heart rate, contractility, and cardiac output during exercise because the sympathetic system drives the exercise cardiac response. Patient B (vagal damage) would actually have a higher resting heart rate but could still increase it somewhat through circulating catecholamines.

quiz_questions.question_text: A comprehensive AP review question asks: A neuron has a resting potential of -70 mV. If you experimentally clamp the membrane at exactly -55 mV (threshold) and hold it there, what ion channels are activated and what would you predict happens next?

options.text: No channels respond at exactly -55 mV; a stronger stimulus is needed
Ca2+ channels open first, followed by Na+ channels, producing a slow depolarization
Voltage-gated Na+ channels open at threshold, creating a positive feedback loop of Na+ influx that rapidly drives the membrane to +30 mV (full depolarization), initiating a complete action potential
Only K+ channels open, and the membrane repolarizes back to -70 mV

quiz_questions.explanation: At threshold (-55 mV), enough voltage-gated Na+ channels open to create a positive feedback loop: Na+ influx depolarizes the membrane further, opening more Na+ channels, causing more depolarization (regenerative cycle), rapidly driving the membrane to the Na+ equilibrium potential near +30 mV and generating a full action potential.
